title: Chat Interface
content: Docs › Using Obsilo › Chat Interface Chat Interface Everything you do in Obsilo starts in the sidebar. Here's how to get the most out of it — from sending your first message to attaching files, using autocomplete, and reading the activity log. Overview The sidebar has three main zones: Header — mode selector, model selector, new chat button Messages area — conversation history with real-time streaming Input area — text input with toolbar (attachments, tools, send) Sending Messages Type your message in the input area at the bottom of the sidebar. Enter — send the message (default) Shift+Enter — insert a newline Ctrl/Cmd+Enter — alternative send key (configurable in Settings → Interface) You can change the send key behavior in Settings → Interface → "Press Enter to send". Context Injection Active File Context Enable "Auto-add active file context" in Settings → Interface to automatically include the note you're currently viewing in every message. A badge in the input area shows the filename. This lets you ask questions like "Summarize this note" or "Find related notes" without having to attach the file manually. Attaching Files Type @ in the input to open the Vault File Picker — a live-search list of all your vault files. Select one (or several) to include their content as context. Attached files appear as chips in the input area and can be removed individually. This is useful when you want the agent to reference a specific note without switching to it. Autocomplete Two shortcut triggers are available in the input: / — opens a list of your workflows and custom prompts. Select one to expand it as a message template. For example, type /daily-review to run your review workflow. @ — opens the vault file picker to attach notes as context. Activity Block When the agent is working, an expandable activity block appears below the spinner. It shows every tool call in real time — the tool name, its inputs, and the result (success or error). You can expand it at any time while the agent is still running. Each tool call shows: Tool name in a monospace badge Key parameters (e.g., the file path for read_file) Diff badge for write operations: +N / -N lines changed Result shown on expansion Task Checklist When the agent calls update_todo_list to plan a complex task, a checklist card appears in the conversation. It updates live as the agent completes steps: [ ] — pending [~] — in progress (highlighted) [x] — complete Approval Flow When the agent wants to create, edit, or delete a file, an approval card appears: Tool name and file path — what will change Diff preview — added (green) and removed (red) lines for edits Approve — execute the operation and continue Reject — block this operation (the agent will be told it was rejected) Auto-approve — approve this operation type for the rest of the session Tip Configure permanent auto-approval in Settings → Permissions to skip the approval card for operations you always trust (e.g., read operations are auto-approved by defau

title: Checkpoints
content: Docs › Reference › Checkpoints Checkpoints Checkpoints give you a safety net for every agent task. Before the first write operation in a task, Obsilo automatically takes a snapshot of all tracked files. If something goes wrong, you can restore to that snapshot with one click. How It Works Obsilo maintains a shadow git repository at: .obsidian/plugins/obsilo-agent/checkpoints/ This is separate from your vault's own git history (if you use git for your vault). It uses isomorphic-git — a pure JavaScript git implementation that requires no native git binary. Checkpoint Lifecycle Task starts — you send a message and the agent begins working First write detected — before executing the first write_file , edit_file , delete_file , or similar operation, Obsilo takes a snapshot of all currently-tracked files Task completes — the checkpoint is retained and listed in Settings → Backup Auto-cleanup (if enabled) — old checkpoints beyond the retention limit are removed What Gets Tracked The shadow repo tracks all files that the agent has read or written in previous tasks. On the first snapshot, it also includes all files in the vault root (excluding ignored paths). This means: Any file the agent has touched can be restored Files in .obsidian-agentignore are not tracked The checkpoint doesn't restore files to a vault-wide snapshot — it restores only the files changed in that task Restoring from a Checkpoint Go to Settings → Advanced → Backup You'll see a list of checkpoints, ordered by date, with the task that created them Click Preview on a checkpoint to see a diff of what changed Click Restore to revert the files changed in that task back to their pre-task state Restore only affects changed files Restoring a checkpoint reverts only the files that were modified in that specific task — not the entire vault. Files created by the task are moved to trash; files edited are restored to their pre-edit content. Configuration In Settings → Vault → Checkpoints : Setting Default Description Enable Checkpoints On Master toggle. When off, no snapshots are taken and restore is unavailable. Checkpoint Timeout 30 seconds Maximum time to wait for a snapshot to complete. If exceeded, the task continues without a checkpoint. Auto-cleanup On Automatically remove old checkpoints to save disk space. Checkpoints vs. Your Vault's Git If you already use git for your vault, you might wonder why Obsilo has its own checkpoint system. The reasons: Non-invasive : Checkpoints don't pollute your vault's git history with agent commits No git required : Works even if you don't use git for your vault at all Automatic : Happens without any user action — no need to remember to commit before each agent task Scoped : Restores only the files a specific task touched, not the whole vault Task-linked : Each checkpoint is linked to the conversation that created it, making it easy to understand what changed and why Disk Usage The shadow repo uses standard git compression. For most vaults, each checkpoin

title: Getting Started
sections: 4. Set Up Semantic Search (Optional but Recommended)
content: Docs › Getting Started Getting Started Obsilo is in early beta and open for testing. It has not been published as Community Plugin yet, hence you'll need to use one of the two other options described below. Install Obsilo Agent, connect your first AI model, and run your first task in under 5 minutes. What you'll be able to do Ask questions about your vault, create and edit notes with AI assistance, research topics on the web and save the results, organize notes in bulk — all from a sidebar inside Obsidian, with a diff preview and approval step before anything changes. Requirements Obsidian Version 1.4.0 or later Platform Desktop only (macOS, Windows, Linux). Not available on mobile. Node.js 18 or later (for building from source) API Key Anthropic, OpenAI, OpenRouter, or a running Ollama instance Installation Install via BRAT (Recommended) BRAT (Beta Reviewers Auto-update Tool) lets you install Obsilo Agent directly from GitHub without building from source, and keeps it up to date automatically. Install BRAT In Obsidian: Settings → Community Plugins → Browse → search for "BRAT" → Install and Enable. Add Obsilo Agent Open BRAT settings → Add Beta Plugin → paste: https://github.com/pssah4/obsilo Click Add Plugin . BRAT downloads and installs the latest release. Enable the plugin Go to Settings → Community Plugins → scroll to "Obsilo Agent" → toggle on. Manual Installation (from source) Clone the repository git clone https://github.com/pssah4/obsilo.git cd obsidian-agent Install dependencies and build npm install npm run build This produces main.js in the project root. Copy to your vault Create the plugin directory and copy the three required files: mkdir -p "/path/to/your-vault/.obsidian/plugins/obsilo-agent" cp main.js styles.css manifest.json \ "/path/to/your-vault/.obsidian/plugins/obsilo-agent/" Enable in Obsidian Open Obsidian → Settings → Community Plugins → scroll to "Obsilo Agent" → toggle it on. Note If you don't see the plugin, make sure "Safe Mode" is turned off in Community Plugins settings. Development mode Run npm run dev to watch for changes and hot-deploy on every save. Set the target vault path in esbuild.config.mjs ( VAULT_PLUGIN_DIR ) to auto-deploy on each build. First Setup 1. Add an AI Model Open Obsidian Settings → Obsilo Agent → Providers → Models . You'll see pre-configured built-in models. Click Edit on any model to add your API key and enable it: Provider Where to get key Recommended model Anthropic console.anthropic.com Claude Sonnet 4.5 OpenAI platform.openai.com GPT-4o OpenRouter openrouter.ai Llama 3.2 3B (free tier) Ollama Run locally: ollama serve llama3.2 or qwen2.5:7b After entering the key, click Test Connection to verify it works. Then enable the model to make it available in the chat. 2. Open the Sidebar Click the Obsilo Agent icon in the Obsidian ribbon (left or right edge of the window). If you don't see it, use the Command Palette ( Ctrl/Cmd+P ) and search for "Open Agent Sidebar". The sideb

title: MCP Servers
content: Docs › Reference › MCP Servers MCP Servers The Model Context Protocol (MCP) lets you connect external tools and data sources to Obsilo Agent. MCP servers expose tools that the agent can call just like built-in tools. What is MCP? MCP is an open protocol for connecting AI models to external tools and resources. An MCP server is a small program that exposes a set of tools (with JSON schemas) and executes them when called. Obsilo connects to MCP servers and makes their tools available to the agent via the use_mcp_tool tool. Examples of what MCP servers can provide: Calendar and task management (Google Calendar, Todoist) Code execution and analysis Database access File system operations outside the vault External API integrations (GitHub, Slack, Notion) Custom automation scripts Supported Transports Transport How it works Use case stdio Obsilo launches a local process and communicates via stdin/stdout Local tools, scripts, CLI programs SSE Connects to a remote server via Server-Sent Events (HTTP GET) Remote services, hosted tools streamable-http Bidirectional HTTP streaming Modern remote MCP servers Adding an MCP Server Go to Settings → Agent Behaviour → MCP and click Add Server . Fill in the server configuration: stdio Server (Local Process) { "type": "stdio", "command": "npx", "args": ["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem", "/Users/me/Documents"], "env": { "MY_SECRET": "value" }, "timeout": 30000, "alwaysAllow": ["read_file", "list_directory"] } SSE Server (Remote) { "type": "sse", "url": "https://my-mcp-server.example.com/sse", "headers": { "Authorization": "Bearer my-token" }, "timeout": 30000 } Streamable HTTP Server { "type": "streamable-http", "url": "https://my-mcp-server.example.com/mcp", "headers": { "Authorization": "Bearer my-token" } } Configuration Fields Field Type Description type string Transport: "stdio", "sse", or "streamable-http" command string (stdio only) The command to run (e.g., "node", "npx", "python") args string[] (stdio only) Command arguments env object (stdio only) Environment variables injected into the process url string (sse/http) Server URL headers object (sse/http) HTTP headers (e.g., Authorization) timeout number Connection timeout in ms (default: 30000) alwaysAllow string[] Tool names on this server that never require user approval disabled boolean Temporarily disable this server without removing it Popular MCP Servers Filesystem Access files outside the vault: name: "filesystem" type: stdio command: npx args: ["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem", "/path/to/allow"] Fetch (Web) Enhanced web fetching with cookie support: name: "fetch" type: stdio command: npx args: ["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-fetch"] GitHub Access GitHub repositories, issues, and PRs: name: "github" type: stdio command: npx args: ["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-github"] env: GITHUB_PERSONAL_ACCESS_TOKEN: "ghp_..." Sqlite Query and update SQLite databases: name: "sqlite" type: stdio command: npx args: ["-y", "@mod

title: Memory & Chat History
content: Docs › Using Obsilo › Memory & Chat History Memory & Chat History Obsilo remembers your conversations and learns your preferences over time. Chat history stores every conversation for browsing and restore. The memory system automatically extracts knowledge from your conversations and personalizes the agent across sessions. Privacy first All memory data is stored locally in your plugin directory. Memory extraction uses a dedicated model you choose (ideally a small, cheap model like Haiku). No data leaves your machine except for the LLM API calls you configure. Chat History Chat history is enabled by default. Every conversation is saved automatically as a JSON file inside the plugin directory ( .obsidian/plugins/obsilo-agent/history/ ). History Panel Click the history icon in the sidebar header (left of the New Chat button) to open the history panel. It slides in from the left and shows your conversations grouped by date: Today / Yesterday / This Week / Older Each entry shows the conversation title, date, and message count Use the text filter at the top to search through titles Hover over an entry to reveal the delete button Restoring a Conversation Click any conversation in the history panel to restore it. The full message history is loaded back into the sidebar — including assistant messages, tool calls, and the internal conversation state. You can continue the conversation exactly where you left off. Auto-Titling After the first assistant response, Obsilo generates a short title (3-8 words) for the conversation using the active model. This title appears in the history panel and helps you find conversations later. Memory System The memory system has two layers: Session memory — a summary of each conversation, stored as a Markdown file Long-term memory — your profile, active projects, and behavioral patterns, distilled from session summaries How It Works You have a conversation with the agent as usual When the conversation ends (new chat or close), if it had enough messages (configurable, default: 6), a background extraction job is queued The extraction model (a separate, cheap model you configure) processes the queue: First, it creates a session summary (key topics, decisions, preferences observed) Then, it checks if any new facts should be promoted to long-term memory (your profile, projects, or patterns) Next time you start a conversation , the agent has your profile, projects, and patterns in its system prompt — plus relevant session summaries retrieved via semantic search Zero impact on chat speed Memory extraction runs entirely in the background after the conversation ends. It never blocks or slows down your active conversation. If Obsidian closes before extraction finishes, the queue is processed on next startup. Memory Files Memory is stored as editable Markdown files in .obsidian/plugins/obsilo-agent/memory/ : File Contains Loaded into context user-profile.md Your name, language, communication style, agent prefer

title: Modes
content: Docs › Using Obsilo › Modes Modes Switch between modes to get the right kind of help for each task — from a read-only question-answering assistant to a fully autonomous agent that can create, edit, and organize your notes. You can also build your own custom modes with their own persona, tool access, and instructions. Built-in Modes Ask Mode Ask is a read-only knowledge assistant. Use it to explore your vault, get synthesized answers from your notes, and find connections — without any risk of changing your files. Try: "What do I know about machine learning?" / "Find notes I haven't updated in 30 days" / "What links to my Project X note?" Access: read, vault intelligence, and semantic search tools. No write access. Agent Mode Agent is a fully autonomous agent with access to all tools. It reads, writes, edits, browses the web, and can spawn sub-agents to parallelize complex tasks. Try: "Create a weekly review template" / "Research X on the web and summarize it to a new note" / "Add a 'status' frontmatter field to all notes in my Projects folder" Access: All tool groups — read, vault, edit, web, MCP, and agent control. Which mode should I use? Start with Ask for exploration and questions. Switch to Agent when you want the AI to take action — create files, edit content, or run complex workflows. Tool Groups Each mode is configured with one or more tool groups . This controls which tools the agent has access to: Group Tools included read read_file , list_files , search_files vault get_frontmatter , search_by_tag , get_vault_stats , get_linked_notes , get_daily_note , open_note , semantic_search , query_base edit write_file , edit_file , append_to_file , create_folder , delete_file , move_file , update_frontmatter , generate_canvas , create_base , update_base web web_fetch , web_search agent ask_followup_question , attempt_completion , update_todo_list , new_task mcp use_mcp_tool Custom Modes Create your own modes in Settings → Agent Behaviour → Modes. Custom modes can be: Vault-scoped — stored in this vault's plugin settings, only available in this vault Global — stored at ~/.obsidian-agent/modes.json , available in all vaults Creating a Custom Mode Click New Mode in the Modes settings tab. Configure: Field Description Slug URL-safe identifier, e.g. researcher or daily-writer Name Display name shown in the mode selector, e.g. "Researcher" Icon Lucide icon name, e.g. search , pen , book Description Short description shown in the mode selector tooltip Role Definition Core system prompt for this mode — defines the agent's persona and behavior Custom Instructions User-editable additional instructions appended to the role definition Tool Groups Which tool categories are available in this mode Scope Vault (this vault only) or Global (all vaults) Example Custom Modes Researcher Slug: researcher Tool Groups: read, vault, web, agent Role Definition: You are a research assistant focused on gathering and synthesizing information. Use semantic_search to find exist

title: Permissions & Safety
content: Docs › Using Obsilo › Permissions & Safety Permissions & Safety You stay in control of every change. By default, Obsilo asks for your approval before creating or editing any file — with a diff preview so you can see exactly what will change before confirming. You can auto-approve trusted operations and protect sensitive folders from access entirely. Safety Layers Approval system — all writes require consent (manual or auto) Checkpoints — automatic git snapshots before first write per task Ignore & protect files — path-based access control Audit log — complete JSONL record of all operations Tool repetition detection — aborts infinite loops Rate limiting & error limits — stop runaway agents Auto-Approval Settings Configure in Settings → Agent Behaviour → Permissions . The master toggle enables or disables the auto-approval system. When disabled, every operation requires manual approval. When enabled, individual categories can be auto-approved: Category Tools covered Default Read read_file , list_files , search_files , and all vault intelligence tools Auto-approved Note Edits write_file , edit_file , append_to_file , update_frontmatter Manual approval Vault Changes create_folder , delete_file , move_file Manual approval Web web_fetch , web_search Manual approval MCP use_mcp_tool Manual approval Mode Switching switch_mode Manual approval Subtasks new_task Manual approval Questions ask_followup_question Auto-approved (shows question directly) Todos update_todo_list Auto-approved Skills Skill injection into system prompt Auto-approved Quick-Toggle Bar A compact permission toggle bar is shown at the top of the chat view. It lets you quickly flip auto-approval on/off for the current session without opening settings. The bar can be hidden in Settings → Permissions → "Show quick-toggle bar in chat". Approval Modal When a write operation requires approval, an approval card appears in the chat: Tool name and file path Diff preview for file edits — added lines in green, removed in red Approve — proceed with the operation Reject — block this operation (the agent is told it was denied) Auto-approve [category] — approve this category for the rest of the session Tip: Trusted workflows If you're running a routine task you trust (like daily note creation), toggle auto-approval for Note Edits on for that session. It will reset when you start a new chat. Vault Access Control Two special files control which paths the agent can access. Place them at the vault root. .obsidian-agentignore Paths matching these gitignore-style patterns are completely off-limits — the agent cannot read or write them. # .obsidian-agentignore # Sensitive personal folders Private/ Finances/** # Old archive — don't touch Archive/2020/ # Specific files Secret-Project.md .obsidian-agentprotected Paths matching these patterns can be read but never written or deleted . # .obsidian-agentprotected # Templates should never be overwritten Templates/ _templates/** # Core reference notes

title: Providers & Models
content: Docs › Reference › Providers & Models Providers & Models Configure the AI models that power Obsilo. You can add multiple models from different providers and switch between them per-chat or per-mode. Supported Providers Provider Type API Key Required Local Anthropic Cloud Yes No OpenAI Cloud Yes No OpenRouter Gateway Yes No Azure OpenAI Cloud (Enterprise) Yes No Ollama Local No Yes LM Studio Local No Yes Custom Any OpenAI-compatible Optional Either Built-in Models Obsilo ships with pre-configured entries for popular models. Enable them in Settings → Providers → Models and add your API key: Anthropic Model ID Display Name Best for claude-opus-4-6 Claude Opus 4.6 Complex reasoning, long tasks claude-sonnet-4-5-20250929 Claude Sonnet 4.5 Balanced capability and speed claude-haiku-4-5-20251001 Claude Haiku 4.5 Fast, lightweight tasks OpenAI Model ID Display Name Best for gpt-4o GPT-4o General purpose, multimodal gpt-4o-mini GPT-4o mini Fast, cost-effective tasks gpt-4.1 GPT-4.1 Latest GPT-4 generation Ollama (Local) Model ID Display Name Notes llama3.2 Llama 3.2 (local) Good general-purpose local model qwen2.5:7b Qwen 2.5 7B (local) Strong at reasoning, multilingual OpenRouter (Free Tier Available) Model ID Display Name Notes anthropic/claude-3.5-sonnet Claude 3.5 Sonnet Via OpenRouter openai/gpt-4o GPT-4o Via OpenRouter meta-llama/llama-3.2-3b-instruct:free Llama 3.2 3B (free) Free tier, no cost Adding a Model Go to Settings → Providers → Models Find the model in the list and click Edit , or click Add Custom Model for a new one Enter your API Key Optionally adjust Temperature (0.0–1.0) and Max Tokens Click Test Connection to verify it works Toggle the model Enabled to make it available in the model selector Provider-Specific Setup Anthropic Get your API key at console.anthropic.com Key format: sk-ant-api03-... No base URL needed (uses default Anthropic endpoint) OpenAI Get your API key at platform.openai.com/api-keys Key format: sk-proj-... or sk-... No base URL needed (uses default OpenAI endpoint) OpenRouter Get your API key at openrouter.ai/keys Base URL is pre-configured: https://openrouter.ai/api/v1 Model IDs follow the format provider/model-name Free models are available — check the OpenRouter catalog Ollama (Local) Install Ollama from ollama.ai Pull a model: ollama pull llama3.2 Start the server: ollama serve (usually starts automatically) In Obsilo: Base URL = http://localhost:11434 , no API key needed Model ID format for Ollama Use the exact model name as shown in ollama list — e.g., llama3.2 , qwen2.5:7b , codellama . LM Studio Download LM Studio from lmstudio.ai Start the local server in LM Studio (usually port 1234) Base URL: http://localhost:1234/v1 Model ID: use the model name shown in LM Studio (e.g., Meta-Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct-GGUF ) Azure OpenAI Base URL format: https://YOUR-RESOURCE.openai.azure.com/openai/deployments/YOUR-DEPLOYMENT API Version: e.g., 2024-10-21 (required for Azure) API Key: your Azure OpenAI key Model ID:

title: Rules, Skills & Workflows
content: Docs › Context & Knowledge › Rules, Skills & Workflows Rules, Skills & Workflows Tailor Obsilo to your vault, writing style, and daily routines. Set permanent conventions the agent always follows, activate context-specific instructions for particular task types, and invoke step-by-step procedures on demand — all without touching the agent's core settings. Mechanism When it applies Best for Rules Always (every message) Permanent conventions the agent must always follow Skills When keywords match user message Context-specific instructions for particular task types Workflows When user types /workflow-name Step-by-step procedures invoked on demand Custom Prompts When user types /prompt-slug Message templates with dynamic variables Rules Rules are Markdown files that are always injected into the agent's system prompt as a RULES section. The agent must follow them in every response. Location <vault>/.obsidian-agent/rules/ Any .md or .txt file in this folder is treated as a rule file. Example Rules Create .obsidian-agent/rules/formatting.md : ## Formatting Conventions - Always use ISO 8601 dates (YYYY-MM-DD) in frontmatter, not natural language dates - Use [[wikilinks]] for internal note references, not markdown links - Every new note must have at least: title, tags, and created date in frontmatter - Headers: ## for main sections, ### for subsections — never use # (reserved for note title) - Tag style: lowercase, hyphenated — use #machine-learning not #MachineLearning Create .obsidian-agent/rules/language.md : Always respond in German. When creating note content, use German unless the existing note content is in another language. Managing Rules Go to Settings → Agent Behaviour → Rules : Create new rule files with the inline editor Toggle individual rules on/off (disabled rules are not injected) Edit existing rule files Delete rule files New rules are enabled by default . Disable them without deleting by toggling them off. Size limit Each rule file is capped at 50,000 characters to prevent unintentionally large system prompts. Skills Skills are modular instruction sets that the agent can apply for specific task types. Unlike rules (which are always active), skills are automatically matched to each message by keyword overlap. Location <vault>/.obsidian-agent/skills/ meeting-notes/ SKILL.md literature-review/ SKILL.md project-planning/ SKILL.md Each skill is a folder containing a SKILL.md file. SKILL.md Format --- name: meeting-notes description: taking meeting notes, capturing action items, agenda, attendees, follow-up --- When writing meeting notes, follow this structure: ## Standard Meeting Note Template Use these sections: - **## Attendees** — list all participants - **## Agenda** — planned topics - **## Notes** — what was discussed - **## Decisions** — explicit decisions made - **## Action Items** — format: `- [ ] @person: task (due: YYYY-MM-DD)` Always link to related projects with [[wikilinks]]. Create a separate action item

title: Semantic Search
content: Docs › Context & Knowledge › Semantic Search Semantic Search Obsilo's semantic search finds notes by meaning , not just keywords — using a local vector index that stays entirely on your machine. How It Works When you build the semantic index, Obsilo: Reads each note in your vault Splits it into chunks (default: ~2000 characters, split at headings) Generates an embedding (a vector of numbers representing the chunk's meaning) for each chunk Stores the vectors in a local HNSW index (Vectra) When you (or the agent) run a search, the query is embedded using the same model, and the nearest vectors are retrieved — finding notes with similar meaning even if they share no keywords with the query. Hybrid Search Obsilo doesn't rely on semantic search alone. Every semantic_search call runs two searches in parallel: Semantic search — vector similarity via Vectra (finds conceptual matches) Keyword search — full-text search via Orama (finds exact word matches) The results are combined using Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) — notes appearing in both lists rise to the top. This means exact names, tags, dates, and codes are found reliably alongside conceptual matches. Graph Augmentation After ranking the top results, Obsilo scans the excerpts for [[wikilinks]] and fetches the first indexed chunk of each linked note that isn't already in the results. These are appended as "linked context" — notes that are intentionally connected to the results but may not have matched semantically themselves. This surfaces the structure of your knowledge graph, not just individual note matches. HyDE (Hypothetical Document Embeddings) HyDE is an optional technique that improves recall for conceptual queries. When enabled, before embedding the user's query, Obsilo asks the LLM to write a short 2-3 sentence note excerpt that would answer the query. That hypothetical excerpt is then embedded instead of the raw query — giving the embedding model a much richer signal to match against. Trade-off: HyDE costs one extra LLM call per search. It's off by default but can improve results noticeably for complex, conceptual questions. Enable it in Settings → Semantic Index → HyDE. Setting Up the Index Step 1: Enable Semantic Index Go to Settings → Obsilo Agent → Semantic Index and toggle "Enable Semantic Index" on. Step 2: Choose an Embedding Model Go to Settings → Providers → Embeddings and select an embedding model. Model Type Quality Notes Xenova/all-MiniLM-L6-v2 Local (ONNX) Good Default. 384 dimensions. No API key needed. ~23MB download on first use. Xenova/bge-small-en-v1.5 Local (ONNX) Better Small but strong. Good for English-heavy vaults. Xenova/multilingual-e5-small Local (ONNX) Good Multilingual support. Use if your vault contains multiple languages. text-embedding-3-small OpenAI API Excellent Requires OpenAI API key. 1536 dimensions. Higher quality, cloud-dependent. Changing the embedding model invalidates the index. If you switch models, rebuild the index from scratch — vectors from d

title: Settings Reference
content: Docs › Reference › Settings Reference Settings Reference Complete reference for every setting in Obsilo Agent. Open via Obsidian Settings → Obsilo Agent. Providers Models Setting Description Active Models list List of all configured models. Each entry shows provider, name, and enabled status. Add Custom Model Add a new model with any provider type. Set name, provider, API key, base URL, temperature, max tokens. Global Custom Instructions Text appended to the system prompt for ALL modes. Use for universal formatting rules, language preferences, etc. Default Model The model selected by default in new chats. Set by enabling and selecting a model. Embeddings Setting Description Embedding Model Model used to generate vectors for semantic search. Changing this requires rebuilding the index. Add Custom Embedding Model Add a Xenova/transformers model by name or an API-based embedding model. Agent Behaviour Modes Setting Description Built-in modes Ask and Agent modes are always present. Their role definitions can be viewed but not edited. Custom Modes list User-created modes (vault or global scope). New Mode Create a custom mode with slug, name, icon, description, role definition, custom instructions, tool groups, and scope. Per-mode Model Override the default model for a specific mode. Per-mode Tools Restrict available tools to a subset of the mode's tool groups. Per-mode MCP Servers Whitelist specific MCP servers for this mode. Forced Skills Skills always injected for this mode regardless of keyword matching. Forced Workflow Workflow applied to every message in this mode (unless message starts with /). Permissions Setting Default Description Enable Auto-Approval Off Master toggle. When off, every operation requires manual approval. Show quick-toggle bar in chat On Display permission toggles at the top of the chat view. Read operations Auto-approved read_file, list_files, search_files, and vault intelligence tools. Note edits Manual write_file, edit_file, append_to_file, update_frontmatter. Vault changes Manual create_folder, delete_file, move_file. Web operations Manual web_fetch, web_search. MCP tools Manual use_mcp_tool. Mode switching Manual switch_mode. Subtasks Manual new_task (spawn sub-agents). Questions Auto-approved ask_followup_question (shows question card directly without approval step). Todo updates Auto-approved update_todo_list. Loop Setting Default Description Consecutive Mistake Limit 3 Stop the agent after N consecutive tool errors. 0 = disabled. Rate Limit (ms) 0 Minimum milliseconds between API iterations. 0 = no limit. Use to avoid rate limit errors. Context Condensing Off Automatically summarize conversation when estimated tokens exceed the threshold. Condensing Threshold 80% % of the model's context window at which to trigger condensing (50–95). Power Steering Frequency 0 Inject a mode-role reminder every N iterations to keep the agent on task. 0 = disabled. Rules Setting Description Rules list All .md and .txt files in .obsidian-

title: Tools Reference
content: Docs › Reference › Tools Reference Tools Reference Complete reference for all 30+ tools available to Obsilo Agent. Tools are grouped by category, which maps to the tool group permission system. Read Tools These tools are read-only and are in the read tool group. Auto-approved by default. read read_file Read the complete content of a file. Always call this before editing an existing file. read list_files List files and folders in a directory. Use "/" for the vault root. Optional recursive parameter. read search_files Full-text and regex search across files. Returns matching lines with file path and line number. Supports file_pattern glob filter. Parameters: read_file Parameter Type Required Description path string yes Vault-relative path to the file (e.g., "Projects/plan.md") Parameters: list_files Parameter Type Required Description path string yes Directory path. Use "/" for vault root recursive boolean no Include subdirectory contents (default: false) Parameters: search_files Parameter Type Required Description path string yes Directory to search in pattern string yes Text or regex pattern to search for file_pattern string no Glob pattern to filter files (e.g., "*.md") Vault Intelligence Tools These tools are read-only metadata and search operations. In the vault tool group. Auto-approved by default. vault get_vault_stats Returns a vault overview: total notes, folder tree, top tags, recently modified files. No parameters needed. vault get_frontmatter Read all YAML frontmatter fields of a note (tags, aliases, dates, custom properties). vault search_by_tag Find all notes with given tags. Supports AND or OR matching. Tags with or without # both work. vault get_linked_notes Get forward links and backlinks for a note. Direction: "both" (default), "forward", or "backlinks". vault get_daily_note Read a daily note. offset=0 (today), -1 (yesterday), 1 (tomorrow). create=true creates it if missing. vault open_note Open a note in the Obsidian editor view. Use after creating or editing a note to bring it into focus. vault semantic_search Hybrid semantic + keyword search. Finds notes by meaning. Requires Semantic Index to be built. Supports folder, tag, and date filters. vault query_base Query an Obsidian Bases (.base) file. Returns the notes matching the view's filter conditions. Parameters: semantic_search Parameter Type Required Description query string yes Natural language search query top_k number no Max results to return (default: 8, max: 20) folder string no Restrict results to notes in this folder (prefix match) tags string[] no Only return notes that have ANY of these tags since string no Only return notes modified on or after this date (ISO format: "2025-01-01") Writing & Editing Tools These tools modify vault files and require approval (or auto-approval). In the edit tool group. edit write_file Create a new file or completely replace an existing file's content. Preferred for new notes or full rewrites. edit edit_file Replace a specific string

title: [Dev] Architecture Overview
content: Architecture >Core Architecture > Architecture Overview Architecture Overview Obsilo is an AI agent built as an Obsidian plugin. It runs entirely in the Electron renderer process, with no backend server, combining an agentic loop with a governed tool execution pipeline and a modular prompt system. System Context Obsilo connects three external boundaries: the Obsidian Vault (local filesystem), LLM API providers (Anthropic, OpenAI, Ollama, etc.), and optionally MCP servers for extended tool access. Quality Goals Priority 1: Data Security - Fail-closed approval, git checkpoints, .obsidian-agentignore. Priority 2: Extensibility - Custom modes, rules, skills, workflows, MCP servers, plugin skills. Priority 3: Privacy - Local-first: embeddings, index, memory all on disk. No telemetry. Priority 4: Transparency - Diff preview, operation log (JSONL), tool cards in UI. Priority 5: Performance - Streaming responses, parallel tool execution, incremental index builds. Key Architectural Decisions 1. Central tool execution pipeline - Every tool call passes through a 6-step governance flow: validate, ignore check, approval, checkpoint, execute, log. 2. Fail-closed approval - If no approval callback is registered, write operations are denied by default. 3. Shadow git repository - isomorphic-git maintains a hidden repo for checkpoint/restore without requiring system git. 4. Mode-based tool filtering - Each mode defines which tool groups are accessible. 5. Local-only semantic index - Vectra HNSW with Xenova embeddings runs entirely on-device. 6. Sliding-window repetition detection - Prevents infinite tool loops by tracking recent calls in a 5-call window. 7. Multi-provider API adapter - Unified ApiHandler interface normalizes Anthropic, OpenAI, Ollama, Azure, and custom providers. 8. 3-tier memory architecture - Chat history, session extraction, long-term memory promotion via LLM pipeline. 9. VaultDNA plugin discovery - Runtime reflection scans installed plugins, generates skill files automatically. 10. Hybrid search with RRF - Semantic + BM25/TF-IDF combined via Reciprocal Rank Fusion (k=60). Building Blocks Core Engine: AgentTask (main agent loop), ToolExecutionPipeline (6-step governance), SystemPrompt (16 modular sections), ToolRegistry (36 tools), ModeService (mode resolution). Knowledge & Memory: SemanticIndexService (Vectra, BM25, RRF, HyDE), MemoryService (6 memory files), ExtractionQueue (background extraction). Governance: IgnoreService (path-based access control), GitCheckpointService (shadow git), OperationLogger (JSONL audit). External Dependencies: @anthropic-ai/sdk, openai, @modelcontextprotocol/sdk, vectra, @xenova/transformers, isomorphic-git.

title: [Dev] Agent Loop
content: Architecture >Core Architecture > Agent Loop Agent Loop The AgentTask class is the central conversation loop. It manages the iterative cycle of sending messages to the LLM, processing tool calls, and accumulating conversation history until a termination condition is reached. AgentTask Lifecycle Constructor receives: api (ApiHandler), toolRegistry, taskCallbacks (14 methods), modeService, consecutiveMistakeLimit, rateLimitMs, condensingEnabled, condensingThreshold, powerSteeringFrequency, maxIterations (default 25), depth, maxSubtaskDepth (default 2). Iteration Flow Each iteration follows 13 sequential steps: 1. Abort check - If abortSignal is set, exit immediately. 2. Mode switch - Apply any pending mode switch. 3. Rate limiting - Delay by rateLimitMs if configured. 4. Power steering - Every N iterations, inject a system reminder. 5. Rebuild prompt cache - Regenerate system prompt with updated context. 6. Stream LLM response - Send conversation history to the API. 7. Accumulate tool calls - Collect tool_use blocks from the stream. 8. Execute tools - Process through ToolExecutionPipeline. PARALLEL_SAFE tools run concurrently. 9. Append results - Add tool results to conversation history. 10. Context condensing - If token estimate exceeds threshold, summarize older messages. 11. Completion check - If attempt_completion was called or no tools invoked, end loop. 12. Repetition detection - Check 5-call sliding window for repeated patterns. 13. Iteration counter - Increment and check against maxIterations. Termination Conditions: Natural end (text only, no tools), Completion signal (attempt_completion), Abort signal, Max iterations (25), Consecutive errors, Repetition loop (3 times in 5-call window). Parallel Tool Execution: Tools in PARALLEL_SAFE set run concurrently via Promise.all(). Includes read_file, list_files, search_files, search_by_tag, get_vault_stats, get_linked_notes, get_daily_note, semantic_search, web_search, web_fetch. ContextExtensions: askQuestion, signalCompletion, onApprovalRequired, updateTodos, switchMode, spawnSubtask, onCheckpoint, invalidateToolCache. AgentTaskCallbacks interface defines 14 callbacks: onIterationStart, onThinking, onText, onToolStart, onToolResult, onUsage, onTodoUpdate, onContextCondensed, onModeSwitch, onCheckpoint, onQuestion, onApprovalRequired, onAttemptCompletion, onComplete, onError.

title: [Dev] System Prompt
content: Architecture >Core Architecture > System Prompt System Prompt Architecture The system prompt is assembled dynamically from 16 modular sections, each managed by a dedicated builder function in src/core/prompts/sections/. The orchestrator buildSystemPromptForMode() concatenates them in a fixed order. Prompt Assembly On every iteration, the system prompt is rebuilt with current context (time, active file, memory, tool definitions). This ensures the LLM always has up-to-date information. The 16 Sections: 1. dateTime - Current date, time, and timezone for time-aware decisions. 2. vaultContext - Vault name, active file path, settings summary, workspace context. 3. capabilities - High-level overview of what the agent can do. 4. memory - Up to 4000 characters from 5 memory files (800 chars each max): soul.md, user-profile.md, projects.md, patterns.md, learnings.md. Each wrapped in XML tags. 5. tools - Complete JSON schema definitions for all tools accessible in the current mode. Largest section. 6. pluginSkills - VaultDNA-discovered plugin skills. One line per plugin. 7. toolRules - Specific usage rules for tools. 8. toolDecisionGuidelines - Decision tree for choosing between similar tools. 9. objective - 11-point task decomposition framework. 10. responseFormat - Response structuring guidelines. 11. explicitInstructions - Best practices and anti-patterns. 12. securityBoundary - The vault is sacred guardrails. 13. modeDefinition - Role definition for the currently active mode. 14. customInstructions - Global + mode-specific user-defined instructions. 15. skills - Content from keyword-matched skill files. 16. rules - Active rules from .obsidian-agent/rules/ wrapped in rule tags. Subtask Mode When a child task is spawned via new_task, it receives a leaner prompt: sections 1-5, 7, 12, and the parent's task description. Skills, rules, and custom instructions are not inherited to keep the subtask focused.

title: [Dev] Tool Execution Pipeline
content: Architecture >Tool System > Tool Execution Pipeline Tool Execution Pipeline Every tool call from the LLM passes through a 6-step governance pipeline before reaching the actual tool implementation. This ensures safety, auditability, and user control. The 6-Step Pipeline: 1. VALIDATE - Tool exists? Input valid? Mode allows it? 2. IGNORE CHECK - IgnoreService: path blocked? 3. APPROVAL - Write operation? Auto-approve config? Show diff, wait for user. 4. CHECKPOINT - GitCheckpointService: snapshot before write. 5. EXECUTE - BaseTool.execute(input, context). 6. LOG - OperationLogger: append to JSONL. ToolRegistry Central Map<string, BaseTool> created once during plugin startup. Categories: Vault Read (3): read_file, list_files, search_files. Vault Write (6): write_to_file, apply_diff, insert_content, move_file, create_directory, delete_file. Vault Intelligence (7): search_by_tag, get_vault_stats, get_linked_notes, get_daily_note, open_note, search_and_replace, batch_rename. Semantic (1): semantic_search. Canvas (1): generate_canvas. Bases (3): query_base, create_base, update_base. Web (2): web_search, web_fetch. Agent Control (5): ask_followup_question, attempt_completion, update_todo_list, switch_mode, new_task. Plugin Skills (3): execute_command, enable_plugin, resolve_capability_gap. Plugin API (2): call_plugin_api, execute_recipe. Settings (2): update_settings, configure_model. MCP (1): use_mcp_tool. BaseTool Interface: abstract class with name, isWriteOperation, execute(input, context), formatSuccess, formatError, formatContent. ToolMeta: group (read|vault|edit|web|agent|mcp|skill), label, description, icon, signature. Tool Group Filtering: expandToolGroups() resolves group names to individual tool names. Groups are hierarchical: read subset of vault subset of edit. Repetition Detection: Sliding window of last 5 tool calls tracks tool name + arguments. Same combination 3 times terminates the loop.

title: [Dev] Governance & Safety
content: Architecture >Tool System > Governance & Safety Governance & Safety Obsilo implements defense in depth with four security layers: path-based access control, user approval, git checkpoints, and audit logging. IgnoreService Path-based access control using .obsidian-agentignore and .obsidian-agentprotected files. Hard-Coded Blocks: .git/ (Git internals), .obsidian/workspace (Obsidian workspace state), .obsidian/workspace.json, .obsidian/cache. Pattern Syntax: Gitignore-style. * matches non-/, ** matches anything, trailing / = directory. ReDoS protection: reject patterns > 200 chars or with 3+ consecutive **. Fail-closed: all paths inaccessible until governance rules load. Auto-Approval System Per-group approval toggles. Default: master toggle disabled. Agent tools always auto-approved: ask_followup_question, attempt_completion, update_todo_list, open_note, update_settings, configure_model. Approval Groups: read (Read operations), noteEdits (apply_diff, insert_content), vaultChanges (write_to_file, move_file, delete_file, create_directory), web (web_search, web_fetch), mcp (use_mcp_tool), mode (switch_mode), subtasks (new_task), skills (execute_command, enable_plugin), pluginApiRead/Write (call_plugin_api), recipes (execute_recipe). Git Checkpoints GitCheckpointService uses isomorphic-git to maintain a shadow git repository. Before every write operation, a snapshot is created. Users can restore to any checkpoint from the UI. Pure-JS, no system git required. Shadow repo at .obsidian/plugins/obsilo-agent/checkpoints/. Auto-snapshot before every write operation. Operation Logger Every tool execution is logged to a JSONL file. Fields: timestamp, tool name, input parameters, result status, duration, mode, conversation ID. Log file: .obsidian/plugins/obsilo-agent/operations.jsonl. Defense in Depth Layer 1: IgnoreService (path-based blocks). Layer 2: Approval System (user consent). Layer 3: Git Checkpoints (undo capability). Layer 4: Operation Logger (full audit trail).

title: [Dev] Semantic Search Pipeline
content: Architecture >Knowledge & Memory > Semantic Search Pipeline Semantic Search Pipeline Obsilo implements hybrid search combining vector similarity (Vectra HNSW) with keyword matching (BM25/TF-IDF) via Reciprocal Rank Fusion, enhanced by HyDE expansion and wikilink graph augmentation. Vectra LocalIndex Pure-TypeScript HNSW vector index. Storage: .obsidian/plugins/obsilo-agent/semantic-index/. Default model: Xenova/all-MiniLM-L6-v2 (384 dimensions, runs locally via ONNX). Two embedding paths: Xenova local (default, zero config) and API-based (OpenAI-compatible endpoint). Heading-Aware Chunking 5-stage chunking pipeline: 1. Split by headings - break document at Markdown heading boundaries. 2. Respect chunk size - default 2000 characters per chunk. 3. 10% overlap - between consecutive chunks for context continuity. 4. Hard split at 70% - to avoid mid-word breaks. 5. Ellipsis prefix - for continued chunks. BM25/TF-IDF with Stemming Tokenization: lowercase + split on non-alphanumeric. Suffix stemmer with rules for English (-ing, -tion, -ness, -ment, -able, -ful, -less, -ous, -ive, -ly, -ed, -er, -est, -es, -s) and German (-ung, -heit, -keit, -isch, -lich, -bar, -sam, -en, -er, -es, -te, -ten). Scoring: TF = matches/total tokens, IDF = log(N/df), Score = SUM(TF * IDF). Hybrid Search & RRF Fusion User query runs two searches in parallel: Semantic Search (Vectra HNSW, top_k * 3) producing Rank List A (by cosine similarity), and BM25 Keyword Search (TF-IDF, stemming) producing Rank List B. RRF Fusion: score(doc) = SUM(1 / (k + rank_i)), k=60. Then Graph Augmentation: 1-hop wikilink expansion (max 5 per hit). HyDE (Hypothetical Document Expansion) When enabled, the LLM generates a hypothetical answer to the query, which is then embedded alongside the original query to improve recall for abstract questions. Incremental Build Index built incrementally: only new/modified files re-indexed. File modification tracked via metadata. Auto-update queue processes changes in background.

title: [Dev] Memory Architecture
content: Architecture >Knowledge & Memory > Memory Architecture Memory Architecture Obsilo implements a 3-tier memory system that enables the agent to learn from conversations: chat history for short-term, session summaries for medium-term, and promoted facts for long-term persistence. 3-Tier Architecture Tier 1: Chat History (ConversationStore) - Full conversations as JSON, per session. Tier 2: Session Summaries (SessionExtractor) - LLM-generated summary, one per conversation. Semantically searchable via MemoryRetriever. Tier 3: Long-Term Memory (LongTermExtractor) - Facts promoted into persistent Markdown files: user-profile.md, projects.md, patterns.md, soul.md, learnings.md. Memory Files Storage: .obsidian/plugins/obsilo-agent/memory/. soul.md (<agent_identity>) - Agent personality, language, values, anti-patterns. user-profile.md (<user_profile>) - User preferences, communication style. projects.md (<active_projects>) - Current projects and contexts. patterns.md (<behavioral_patterns>) - Learned interaction patterns. learnings.md (<task_learnings>) - Task execution insights. knowledge.md - On-demand via semantic search only. Limits: 800 characters per file, 4000 total in system prompt. ExtractionQueue Persistent FIFO queue surviving Obsidian restarts. Queue file: .obsidian/plugins/obsilo-agent/pending-extractions.json. Queue items: conversationId, transcript, title, queuedAt, type ('session' | 'long-term'). Two async LLM calls per conversation: session extraction + long-term promotion. Memory Context Injection Each memory file is injected into the system prompt wrapped in its XML tag. The buildMemoryContext() method reads all files, truncates to limits, and concatenates. OnboardingService 5-step setup flow for new users. Creates default soul.md with name Obsilo, language Deutsch, and anti-patterns.

title: [Dev] Mode System
content: Architecture >Extensions > Mode System Mode System Modes define the agent's role, accessible tools, and behavioral constraints. The ModeService resolves modes from a three-tier hierarchy and filters the tool registry accordingly. Mode Resolution Three-tier priority: built-in modes, global modes (~/.obsidian-agent/modes.json), vault modes (per-vault settings). Vault modes override built-in modes with the same slug. Built-in Modes Ask (slug: ask) - Tool Groups: read, vault, agent. Role: Read-only Q&A assistant. Agent (slug: agent) - Tool Groups: read, vault, edit, web, agent, mcp, skill. Role: Full-access agent with write capabilities. Custom Modes ModeConfig interface: slug, name, roleDefinition, toolGroups, customInstructions, modelOverride, mcpWhitelist. Users can create custom modes with specific tool access, custom role definitions, and per-mode model overrides. Tool Group Mapping expandToolGroups() resolves group names to individual tool names. Groups are hierarchical: read is a subset of vault, which is a subset of edit. Mode Switching The switch_mode tool allows the LLM to change modes during a conversation. The switch takes effect at the start of the next iteration. Rules System Rules stored at .obsidian-agent/rules/. Each .md file is a rule with on/off toggle. Max file size: 50,000 chars. Active rules injected into system prompt section 16 wrapped in <rule> tags. Workflows Stored at .obsidian-agent/workflows/. Slug derived from filename. Activated via /slug slash commands. Workflow content wrapped in <explicit_instructions> tags. Skills Keyword-matched content injected into system prompt section 15. Skills auto-activate when user message contains matching keywords.

title: [Dev] MCP Client
content: Architecture >Extensions > MCP Client MCP Client The McpClient manages connections to external MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers, providing the agent with access to tools beyond the built-in set. It supports three transport mechanisms and includes security validation for command execution. McpClient Architecture McpClient maintains a Map<serverId, McpConnection> with status (connected, connecting, disconnected, error), client (MCP SDK Client), and tools (McpToolInfo[]). Methods: connect(serverConfig), connectAll(configs[]), callTool(server, tool, args), disconnect(serverId). Transport Types stdio - Stdin/stdout of a child process for local CLI tools. sse - HTTP Server-Sent Events for remote servers with streaming. streamable-http - HTTP with streaming support for modern HTTP servers. Command Validation For stdio transport, the command string is validated against shell metacharacters to prevent injection. Blocked characters: ; & | ` $ ( ) { } [ ] < > \. This prevents commands like npx some-tool; rm -rf / from executing. Connection Flow 1. Validate config - Check transport type, validate command (stdio) or URL (sse/http). 2. Create transport - Instantiate the appropriate MCP SDK transport. 3. Connect with timeout - Race connection against configurable timeout (default: 60 seconds). 4. Discover tools - Call listTools() on the connected server. 5. Store connection - Save with status, client, and discovered tools. UseMcpToolTool (LLM Bridge) The use_mcp_tool tool bridges LLM tool calls to MCP server invocations. Parameters: server_name, tool_name, arguments. Write operation classification: if tool name contains write, create, delete, update, edit, modify, remove, insert, or set, it requires approval. Per-Mode Server Whitelist Each mode can define which MCP servers are accessible. Empty array means all servers accessible. Error Handling: Connection timeout sets status to error. Server disconnect triggers auto-reconnect on next call. Empty response returns (no output). Non-text response returns (non-text response).

title: [Dev] API Providers
content: Architecture >Extensions > API Providers API Providers Obsilo uses an adapter pattern to support multiple LLM providers through a unified ApiHandler interface. Two concrete implementations handle all 7 provider types: AnthropicProvider for the Anthropic API, and OpenAiProvider for all OpenAI-compatible APIs. ApiHandler Interface createMessage(systemPrompt, messages, tools) returns AsyncGenerator<StreamChunk>. getModel() returns the model identifier string. Stream Chunk Types Both providers normalize their streams into 4 chunk types: text (incremental text tokens), thinking (extended thinking content, Anthropic models), tool_use (complete tool call object after full JSON accumulated), usage (token counts at end of response). AnthropicProvider Uses the official @anthropic-ai/sdk with dangerouslyAllowBrowser: true (required for Electron renderer). Native SDK streaming with content block events. Tool accumulation via Map<number, { id, name, inputJson }> keyed by content block index. Thinking blocks mapped to separate accumulator. Temperature default 0.2, clamped to max 1.0. OpenAiProvider Covers 6 OpenAI-compatible provider types: OpenAI (api.openai.com/v1, Bearer token), Ollama (localhost:11434/v1, no auth), LM Studio (localhost:1234/v1, no auth), OpenRouter (openrouter.ai/api/v1, Bearer + HTTP-Referer + X-Title), Azure (custom deployment URL, api-key header + api-version query), Custom (user-configured URL, Bearer token). SSE stream parsing: Manual parsing of data: lines. Temperature omitted for O-series models. Azure uses max_completion_tokens instead of max_tokens. Internal Message Format All providers normalize to Anthropic's content block format: MessageParam with role (user|assistant) and content (string or ContentBlock[]). ContentBlock types: text, image, tool_use, tool_result. Provider Factory buildApiHandlerForModel() reads model configuration and instantiates the appropriate handler.

title: [Dev] VaultDNA
content: Architecture >Extensions > VaultDNA VaultDNA VaultDNA is the plugin discovery system that automatically scans all installed Obsidian plugins, classifies their capabilities, and generates skill files that enable the agent to use any plugin's commands and APIs. Plugin Discovery On plugin start, VaultDNA Scanner enumerates all installed plugins (core + community). For each plugin: read manifest (name, description, version), discover commands (app.commands), classify as FULL / PARTIAL / NONE, API reflection (Tier 2, if available), generate skill file. Continuous sync with 5s polling detects enable/disable/install/remove. Classification System FULL (3+ meaningful commands) - Plugin fully usable by the agent. PARTIAL (1-2 meaningful commands) - Limited functionality available. NONE (0 meaningful commands) - Plugin not directly usable. UI-only commands excluded: toggle, show-, focus, settings, -panel, -sidebar, -pane. API Discovery (Reflection) For plugins with public API via plugin.api, VaultDNA performs runtime reflection: enumerate all methods, filter blocked methods (constructor, execute, executeJs, render, register, unregister, onload, onunload, destroy, eval), filter private methods (starting with _), record method names and parameter signatures. Skill File Generation Each plugin gets a Markdown skill file at .obsidian-agent/plugin-skills/{plugin-id}.skill.md with frontmatter (name, id, version, classification, commands, apiMethods) and sections for Commands and API Methods. Settings Sanitization When reading plugin settings, sensitive fields are automatically redacted. Sensitive patterns: api-key, secret, password, token, credential, oauth. Internal state excluded: lastSync, cache, version. Size limits: strings truncated at 500 chars, arrays show 3-item preview, max depth 3. Total output cap: 8,000 characters. Plugin Tools execute_command (run any Obsidian command by ID), enable_plugin (enable a disabled plugin), resolve_capability_gap (find plugins for a needed capability), call_plugin_api (call a method on a plugin's public API), execute_recipe (run a multi-step recipe). Continuous Sync Polling every 5 seconds detects plugin enable/disable changes. On change, affected skill files are regenerated with a 3-second reclassification delay.

title: [Dev] UI Architecture
content: Architecture >UI > UI Architecture UI Architecture Obsilo's UI is built as an Obsidian ItemView registered in the right sidebar. The AgentSidebarView orchestrates message rendering, user input, autocomplete, and all interactive elements. AgentSidebarView Extends Obsidian's ItemView (view type: obsidian-agent-sidebar, display name: Obsilo Agent). State Management: conversationHistory (MessageParam[]), uiMessages (UIMessage[]), activeConversationId, currentAbortController, lastUserMessage (for retry), nextMessageHidden. Message Flow User types message, AutocompleteHandler checks for triggers (/, @), message submitted. Create AbortController, render user bubble, instantiate AgentTask, run task. Callbacks: onText (append to assistant bubble), onThinking (render thinking block), onToolStart/onToolResult (render/update tool card), onApprovalRequired (show diff modal), onQuestion (show question dialog), onTodoUpdate (update todo box), onCheckpoint (show undo bar), onUsage (update token display), onComplete (finalize, save to store). Rendering Components Message Bubbles rendered as markdown via Obsidian MarkdownRenderer.render(). Tool Cards: collapsible cards with tool name, badge (group color), input parameters, result. Read-only tools with same group merged. Thinking Blocks: distinct collapsible with muted styling. Diff-Stats Badge: lines added/removed after write operations. Token Usage: HH:MM, input/output counts. Autocomplete Handler Two triggers: / (workflows + custom prompts), @ (vault files via VaultFilePicker). VaultFilePicker Live search across all vault markdown files. Max 10 results. Active note pinned at top. Keyboard navigation and multi-select. ToolPickerPopover Session-level tool configuration with groups for Built-In, MCP Servers, Skills, Workflows. HistoryPanel Overlay showing past conversations with search, restore, and delete. Approval Flow DiffReviewModal (full diff with syntax highlighting) and ApproveEditModal (simplified approve/reject). Both return Promise<boolean>. Settings UI 16-tab settings interface built with Obsidian PluginSettingTab.
